## Implementation (from contracts/dsl_operations.ex)
```elixir
@doc """
Connect to a bidirectional WebSocket endpoint for speech-to-speech AI.

## Options
- :headers - List of {name, value} tuples for authentication
- :callback_module - Module implementing WsBidirectional.Callback
- :inbound_format - Audio format from WebSocket (:pcm_16le, :pcmu, :opus)
- :outbound_format - Audio format to WebSocket (:pcm_16le, :pcmu, :opus)
- :sample_rate - Sample rate in Hz (default: 16000)

## Example
    call
    |> connect_bidirectional_ws("wss://api.openai.com/v1/realtime",
        headers: [{"Authorization", "Bearer \#{api_key}"}],
        callback_module: MyApp.AIHandler
      )
"""
@spec connect_bidirectional_ws(t(), String.t(), keyword()) :: t()
def connect_bidirectional_ws(%__MODULE__{} = call, url, opts \\ []) when is_binary(url) do
  operation = {:connect_bidirectional_ws, url, opts}
  %{call | __operations__: call.__operations__ ++ [operation]}
end
```

title: [US1] Add execute_connect_bidirectional_ws handler in ActionExecutor
description:
Add the handler that executes the connect_bidirectional_ws operation.

## Context
ActionExecutor processes operations from Call.__operations__ and executes them.

## File to Modify
`apps/parrot/lib/parrot/bridge/action_executor.ex`

## Implementation
```elixir
# Add to @type operation
| {:connect_bidirectional_ws, String.t(), keyword()}

# Add handler clause
defp execute_operation({:connect_bidirectional_ws, url, opts}, call, context) do
  case execute_connect_bidirectional_ws(call, context, url, opts) do
    {:ok, updated_call} -> {:ok, updated_call, :continue}
    error -> error
  end
end

@doc """
Execute the connect_bidirectional_ws operation.

1. Validate no existing bidirectional connection for this call
2. Create WsBidirectional.Config from opts
3. Start WsBidirectionalConnector
4. Store connector PID in call state
"""
@spec execute_connect_bidirectional_ws(Call.t(), context(), String.t(), keyword()) :: execute_result()
def execute_connect_bidirectional_ws(call, _context, url, opts) do
  # Check for existing connection
  case call.__bidirectional_ws_pid__ do
    nil ->
      # Build config
      config = ParrotMedia.WsBidirectional.Config.new!(
        connection_id: call.id <> "_bidir",
        url: url,
        headers: Keyword.get(opts, :headers, []),
        callback_module: Keyword.get(opts, :callback_module),
        callback_state: Keyword.get(opts, :callback_state, %{}),
        inbound_format: Keyword.get(opts, :inbound_format, :pcm_16le),
        outbound_format: Keyword.get(opts, :outbound_format, :pcm_16le),
        sample_rate: Keyword.get(opts, :sample_rate, 16000)
      )
      
      # Start connection
      case ParrotMedia.WsBidirectional.start_link(config) do
        {:ok, pid} ->
          updated_call = %{call | __bidirectional_ws_pid__: pid}
          {:ok, updated_call}
        {:error, reason} ->
          {:error, {:bidirectional_connect_failed, reason}}
      end
      
    _pid ->
      {:error, :already_connected}
  end
end
```

## Reference
- Contracts: specs/004-bidirectional-ws/contracts/dsl_operations.ex (ActionExecutor section)
- Pattern: apps/parrot/lib/parrot/bridge/action_executor.ex

```elixir
  use GenServer
  require Logger

  alias ParrotMedia.WsBidirectional.{Config, Connection}

    inbound_muted: false,

    frames_received: 0,
    frames_dropped: 0,
    reconnect_count: 0,
    buffer: :queue.new(),
    buffer_size: 0,
    connected_at: nil
  ]

  def start_link(%Config{} = config) do
    GenServer.start_link(__MODULE__, config, name: via_tuple(config.connection_id))
  end

  defp via_tuple(connection_id) do
    {:via, Registry, {ParrotMedia.BidirectionalRegistry, {:bidirectional, connection_id}}}
  end

  @impl GenServer
  def init(config) do
    # Start Fresh WebSocket connection
    # Register source/sink PIDs when they connect
    {:ok, %__MODULE__{config: config}}
  end

  # Handle audio from sink -> forward to WebSocket
  @impl GenServer
  def handle_cast({:audio_frame, data}, state) do
    # Forward to WebSocket unless muted
    {:noreply, state}
  end

  # Handle WebSocket events from Connection module
  @impl GenServer
  def handle_info({:ws_connected}, state)
  def handle_info({:ws_disconnected, reason}, state)
  def handle_info({:ws_audio, data}, state)
  def handle_info({:ws_message, data}, state)
```

title: [US1] Implement WsBidirectionalSource Membrane element with jitter buffer
description:
Implement the Membrane Source element that receives audio FROM WebSocket INTO pipeline.

## Context
Source receives audio from connector and pushes to pipeline with jitter buffering.

## File to Create
`apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/ws_bidirectional/source.ex`

## Implementation (from research.md R1, R7)
```elixir
defmodule ParrotMedia.WsBidirectional.Source do
  use Membrane.Source
  require Logger

  def_output_pad :output, accepted_format: _any, availability: :always

  def_options [
    connector_pid: [spec: pid()],
    jitter_buffer_ms: [spec: pos_integer(), default: 60]
  ]

  @impl true
  def handle_init(_ctx, opts) do
    state = %{
      connector_pid: opts.connector_pid,
      jitter_buffer_ms: opts.jitter_buffer_ms,
      buffer: :queue.new(),
      buffer_depth: 0,
      min_buffer_depth: 3,  # Start playback after 3 frames (60ms at 20ms/frame)
      started: false,
      pts: 0
    }
    {[], state}
  end

  @impl true
  def handle_info({:ws_audio, data}, ctx, state) do
    # Add to jitter buffer
    buffer = :queue.in(data, state.buffer)
    depth = state.buffer_depth + 1

    state = %{state | buffer: buffer, buffer_depth: depth}

    # Check if we should start playback or output
    cond do
      not state.started and depth >= state.min_buffer_depth ->
        # Start playback
        {buffers, new_state} = drain_buffer(state)
        {[buffer: {:output, buffers}], %{new_state | started: true}}

      state.started and depth > 0 ->
        # Output buffered audio
        {buffers, new_state} = drain_buffer(state)
        {[buffer: {:output, buffers}], new_state}

      true ->
        {[], state}
    end
  end

  defp drain_buffer(state) do
    # Convert queue to list of Membrane.Buffer structs
    # Calculate PTS for each buffer
  end
end
```

## Jitter Buffer Behavior (from research.md R7)
- Configurable size (default: 60ms = 3 frames at 20ms)
- Ring buffer using Erlang's :queue
- Start playback only after minimum depth reached
- Drop oldest frames on overflow (backpressure strategy per clarification)

## Reference
- Research: specs/004-bidirectional-ws/research.md (R1, R7)
- Data Model: specs/004-bidirectional-ws/data-model.md (jitter_buffer_ms config)

title: [US1] Implement start_link/disconnect in WsBidirectional public API
description:
Implement the core lifecycle functions in the public API module.

## Context
Replace stub implementations with working code that delegates to Connector.

## File to Modify
`apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/ws_bidirectional.ex`

## Implementation
```elixir
defmodule ParrotMedia.WsBidirectional do
  alias ParrotMedia.WsBidirectional.{Config, Connector}

  @spec start_link(Config.t()) :: {:ok, pid()} | {:error, term()}
  def start_link(%Config{} = config) do
    case Config.validate(config) do
      :ok -> Connector.start_link(config)
      {:error, reason} -> {:error, {:invalid_config, reason}}
    end
  end

  @spec disconnect(connection_ref()) :: :ok | {:error, :not_found}
  def disconnect(ref) when is_pid(ref) do
    GenServer.stop(ref, :normal)
    :ok
  catch
    :exit, {:noproc, _} -> {:error, :not_found}
  end

  def disconnect(connection_id) when is_binary(connection_id) do
    case whereis(connection_id) do
      {:ok, pid} -> disconnect(pid)
      error -> error
    end
  end

  @spec whereis(String.t()) :: {:ok, pid()} | {:error, :not_found}
  def whereis(connection_id) do
    case Registry.lookup(ParrotMedia.BidirectionalRegistry, {:bidirectional, connection_id}) do
      [{pid, _}] -> {:ok, pid}
      [] -> {:error, :not_found}
    end
  end
end
```

## Reference
- Contracts: specs/004-bidirectional-ws/contracts/connector_api.ex

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] disconnect stops Connector gracefully

Complete the public API with audio sending and status querying.

## File to Modify
`apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/ws_bidirectional.ex`

## Implementation
```elixir
@spec send_audio(connection_ref(), binary(), keyword()) :: :ok | {:error, term()}
def send_audio(ref, audio_data, opts \\ []) do
  with {:ok, pid} <- resolve_ref(ref) do
    GenServer.cast(pid, {:audio_frame, audio_data, opts})
    :ok
  end
end

@spec status(connection_ref()) :: {:ok, map()} | {:error, :not_found}
def status(ref) do
  with {:ok, pid} <- resolve_ref(ref) do
    GenServer.call(pid, :status)
  end
end

@spec connected?(connection_ref()) :: boolean() | {:error, :not_found}
def connected?(ref) do
  case status(ref) do
    {:ok, %{connection_state: :connected}} -> true
    {:ok, _} -> false
    error -> error
  end
end

defp resolve_ref(pid) when is_pid(pid), do: {:ok, pid}
defp resolve_ref(id) when is_binary(id), do: whereis(id)
```

- Contracts: specs/004-bidirectional-ws/contracts/connector_api.ex

A developer needs visibility into WebSocket connection state to handle errors gracefully, implement retry logic, and provide appropriate user feedback.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. connected event callback invoked on successful connection
2. disconnected event callback invoked with reason on unexpected disconnect
3. reconnecting event callback invoked with attempt number during retry
4. failed event callback invoked when max retries exceeded
5. Non-audio WebSocket messages (JSON) forwarded to callback

## Independent Test
Simulate connection drops and verify callbacks invoked with correct events.

## Key Components
- Callback notification system in WsBidirectionalConnector
- Reconnection logic with configurable retry policy
- WS text/JSON message forwarding

- apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/ws_bidirectional/connector.ex
- apps/parrot_media/test/parrot_media/ws_bidirectional/connector_test.exs

description:
Add mute/unmute functions to WsBidirectional module.

## File
apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/ws_bidirectional.ex

## Implementation
@spec mute(direction(), connection_ref()) :: :ok | {:error, :not_found}
def mute(direction, ref) when direction in [:inbound, :outbound] do
  with {:ok, pid} <- resolve_ref(ref) do
    GenServer.cast(pid, {:mute, direction})
    :ok
  end
end

@spec unmute(direction(), connection_ref()) :: :ok | {:error, :not_found}
def unmute(direction, ref) when direction in [:inbound, :outbound] do
  with {:ok, pid} <- resolve_ref(ref) do
    GenServer.cast(pid, {:unmute, direction})
    :ok
  end
end

title: [US4] Implement terminate cleanup in Connector
description:
Clean up WebSocket and resources in terminate callback.

## File
apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/ws_bidirectional/connector.ex

## Implementation
@impl GenServer
def terminate(reason, state) do
  Logger.info("[WsBidirectional] Terminating: #{inspect(reason)}")
  
  # Close WebSocket if connected
  if state.conn_pid && Process.alive?(state.conn_pid) do
    Fresh.close(state.conn_pid)
  end
  
  # Notify callback
  if state.config.callback_module do
    state.config.callback_module.terminate(reason, state.callback_state)
  end
  
  :ok
end

description:
Create telemetry helper module with event definitions.

## File
apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/ws_bidirectional/telemetry.ex

## Implementation
defmodule ParrotMedia.WsBidirectional.Telemetry do
  @prefix [:parrot_media, :bidirectional_ws]

  def emit_connect_start(metadata) do
    :telemetry.execute(@prefix ++ [:connect, :start], %{}, metadata)
  end

  def emit_connect_stop(metadata, duration_ms) do
    :telemetry.execute(@prefix ++ [:connect, :stop], %{duration_ms: duration_ms}, metadata)
  end

  def emit_disconnect(metadata) do
    :telemetry.execute(@prefix ++ [:disconnect], %{}, metadata)
  end

  def emit_reconnect(metadata, attempt) do
    :telemetry.execute(@prefix ++ [:reconnect], %{attempt: attempt}, metadata)
  end

  def emit_audio_stats(metadata, measurements) do
    :telemetry.execute(@prefix ++ [:audio, :stats], measurements, metadata)
  end

  def emit_error(metadata) do
    :telemetry.execute(@prefix ++ [:error], %{}, metadata)
  end
end

title: Epic: TTS Feature - US3 Provider Profiles
description: type: epic

title: Early Media (183 Session Progress)
description:
# Early Media (183 Session Progress)

Implement early media support - send/receive audio before call is answered via 183 Session Progress with SDP.

## Business Value
- Enables ringback tones from remote party
- IVR prompts before answer (save answer supervision costs)
- Carrier announcements (number not in service, etc.)
- Required for proper PSTN interop

## What is Early Media?

Early media allows bidirectional audio BEFORE the call is answered (200 OK). The 183 Session Progress response contains SDP, establishing media while dialog is in "early" state.

```
UAC                                UAS
 |                                  |
 |----------- INVITE ------------->|
 |<-------- 100 Trying ------------|
 |<--- 183 Session Progress (SDP)--|  <- Early dialog, media starts
 |============ RTP ===============>|
 |<============ RTP ===============|  <- Ringback/IVR plays
 |<---------- 200 OK --------------|  <- Dialog confirmed
 |------------ ACK --------------->|
```

## DSL API

### Receiving Early Media (UAC)
```elixir
defmodule MyHandler do
  use Parrot.InviteHandler

  # Called when 183 received with SDP
  def handle_early_media(response, call) do
    # Early media is now flowing
    # Could play local ringback until remote audio detected
    {:noreply, call}
  end
end
```

### Sending Early Media (UAS)
```elixir
def handle_invite(invite) do
  invite
  |> early_media()  # Sends 183 with SDP, starts media
  |> play("please-wait.wav")
  # ... later ...
  |> answer()  # Sends 200 OK
end
```

## Operations
- early_media() - Send 183 with SDP, start media before answer
- early_media(sdp_opts) - With custom SDP options

## Callbacks
- handle_early_media(response, call) - UAC received 183 with SDP
- handle_early_media_timeout(call) - No early media, no answer

## Implementation Notes

1. Early dialog state in DialogStatem (:early vs :confirmed)
2. MediaSession must support early start (before ACK)
3. Reliable provisional responses (RFC 3262 PRACK) - optional but recommended
4. P-Early-Media header for gateway scenarios (RFC 5009)

## SDP in 183

Same as 200 OK SDP, but:
- May use different port (some implementations)
- Direction typically sendrecv or sendonly
- Establishes media path before answer

## Dependencies
- Requires: MediaSession early start capability
- Parallel with: EPIC-DSL-001, EPIC-DSL-002

## References
- RFC 3261 Section 13.2.2.4 (Early Media)
- RFC 3262: PRACK (reliable provisionals)
- RFC 5009: P-Early-Media Header

title: Support early dialog state in DialogStatem
description:
Ensure DialogStatem properly handles :early state in apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/dialog_statem.ex

RFC 3261 Section 13.2.2.4 - Early Dialog

```elixir
# Handle 183 Session Progress with SDP -> enter early state
def trying(:cast, {:response, %Message{status: 183} = response}, data) 
    when has_sdp?(response) do
  # Create early dialog
  early_dialog = create_dialog_from_response(response, data)
  notify_handler({:early_media, response}, data)
  {:next_state, :early, %{data | dialog: early_dialog}}
end

# Media can flow in :early state
# Early dialog confirmed by 2xx -> :confirmed
def early(:cast, {:response, %Message{status: status} = response}, data)
    when status >= 200 and status < 300 do
  notify_handler({:answer, response}, data)
  {:next_state, :confirmed, data}
end
```

Acceptance:
- 183 with SDP creates early dialog
- Handler notified of early media
- Transition from :early to :confirmed on 2xx
- Early dialog can receive UPDATE
- Unit tests for state transitions

title: Add early_media operation to Parrot.Call (UAS)
description:
Add DSL operation for sending early media in apps/parrot/lib/parrot/call.ex

For UAS (incoming calls) to send 183 with SDP and start media before answer.

```elixir
@doc """
Send 183 Session Progress with SDP and start media.

Use this to play prompts or IVR before answering the call.
Saves answer supervision costs while still interacting with caller.

## Example

    def handle_invite(invite) do
      invite
      |> early_media()  # Sends 183, starts media
      |> play("please-wait.wav")
      # ... do work ...
      |> answer()  # Sends 200 OK
    end
"""
@spec early_media(t(), keyword()) :: t()
def early_media(%__MODULE__{} = call, opts \\ [])
```

Options:
- :sdp_opts - Custom SDP options

Acceptance:
- early_media/1 sends 183 with SDP
- Media session starts in early state
- Can play audio before answer
- answer/1 sends 200 OK and confirms dialog
- Documentation with examples
- Unit tests

title: Handle early media in UAC (outbound calls)
description:
Handle receiving 183 with SDP for outbound calls.

When we make an outbound call and receive 183 Session Progress with SDP,
we need to:
1. Process the SDP and start receiving media
2. Notify the handler so they can stop local ringback
3. Continue waiting for 200 OK

Files:
- apps/parrot/lib/parrot/bridge/handler.ex
- apps/parrot/lib/parrot/call/server.ex

```elixir
# In Bridge.Handler or Call.Server
def handle_response(%Message{status: 183} = response, state) when has_sdp?(response) do
  # Process early SDP
  {:ok, _} = MediaSession.process_early_answer(state.media_session_id, response.body)
  
  # Notify handler
  invoke_callback(state.call, :handle_early_media, [response])
  
  {:ok, state}
end
```

Acceptance:
- 183 with SDP processed correctly
- Media session starts receiving
- Handler callback invoked
- Local ringback can be stopped
- 200 OK still handled correctly after 183
- Integration tests

title: Add handle_early_media callback to InviteHandler
description:
Add callback to apps/parrot/lib/parrot/invite_handler.ex

```elixir
@doc """
Called when 183 Session Progress with SDP is received (UAC side).

Early media means the remote party is sending audio before answering.
This is typically ringback tone or IVR prompts.

## Example

    def handle_early_media(response, call) do
      # Stop local ringback - remote is sending audio
      call |> stop_playback()
      {:noreply, call}
    end
"""
@callback handle_early_media(response :: map(), call :: Call.t()) ::
  {:noreply, Call.t()}

# Default: do nothing (let early audio play)
def handle_early_media(_response, call), do: {:noreply, call}
```

Acceptance:
- Callback defined in behaviour
- Default implementation provided
- Documentation with example
- Callback invoked on 183 with SDP

title: Support early media in MediaSession
description:
Allow MediaSession to start before call is answered.

File: apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/media_session.ex

```elixir
@doc """
Process early SDP answer (from 183 Session Progress).

Unlike process_answer/2 which expects the call to be answered,
this starts media in "early" state where we receive but may
handle things differently.
"""
@spec process_early_answer(session_id(), String.t()) :: {:ok, map()} | {:error, term()}
def process_early_answer(session_id, sdp_answer)

# May also need early offer for UAS:
@spec create_early_offer(session_id(), keyword()) :: {:ok, String.t()} | {:error, term()}
def create_early_offer(session_id, opts \\ [])
```

State tracking:
- Session needs to track if it's in early or confirmed state
- Pipeline may behave differently (e.g., no RTCP until confirmed)

Acceptance:
- process_early_answer/2 starts media from 183
- create_early_offer/2 for UAS 183 response
- State tracked correctly
- Transition to confirmed state on answer
- Unit tests

title: Implement early_media in ActionExecutor
description:
Add execution logic in apps/parrot/lib/parrot/call/action_executor.ex

```elixir
def execute(:early_media, state) do
  # 1. Create SDP offer for 183
  {:ok, sdp} = MediaSession.create_early_offer(state.media_session_id)
  
  # 2. Send 183 Session Progress
  ParrotSip.Transaction.send_response(state.transaction_id, 183, "Session Progress", sdp)
  
  # 3. Start media in early state
  MediaSession.start_early_media(state.media_session_id)
  
  # 4. Mark state as early
  {:ok, %{state | early_media: true}}
end

# When answer() is called after early_media()
def execute(:answer, %{early_media: true} = state) do
  # Just send 200 OK - media already flowing
  ParrotSip.Transaction.send_response(state.transaction_id, 200, "OK", state.sdp)
  {:ok, %{state | answered: true, early_media: false}}
end
```

Acceptance:
- early_media sends 183 with SDP
- Media starts in early state
- answer after early_media just sends 200
- State transitions correct
- Integration tests

- Audio verified during early state

title: Implement RTP Forwarding for B2BUA
description:
## Problem
B2BUA MediaBridge has TODO comments - RTP forwarding not actually implemented. Cannot bridge media between call legs.

## Root Cause
media_bridge.ex has 4 TODO comments (lines 159, 213, 260, 291) all saying "When T08 is implemented". Configuration is stored but never used.

## Files to Modify
- `/apps/parrot/lib/parrot/bridge/media_bridge.ex` - Implement forwarding logic
- `/apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/media_session.ex` - Add RTP forward API
- Pipelines - Add forwarding elements

## Implementation (Elixir Best Practices)

### RTP Forwarding Architecture
```
A-leg RTP ──► A-leg Pipeline ──► Forward Tee ──► B-leg Pipeline ──► B-leg RTP
                    ▲                                   │
                    └───────────────────────────────────┘
                              (bidirectional)
```

### MediaSession API
```elixir
@spec set_rtp_forward(session_id(), forward_config()) :: :ok | {:error, term()}
def set_rtp_forward(session_id, %{target_session_id: target_id}) do
  GenServer.call(via_tuple(session_id), {:set_rtp_forward, target_id})
end

@spec pause_rtp_forward(session_id()) :: :ok
def pause_rtp_forward(session_id) do
  GenServer.call(via_tuple(session_id), :pause_rtp_forward)
end

@spec resume_rtp_forward(session_id()) :: :ok
def resume_rtp_forward(session_id) do
  GenServer.call(via_tuple(session_id), :resume_rtp_forward)
end
```

### Pipeline Forwarding Element
Add Membrane Tee element to split RTP for local playback + forwarding:
```elixir
# In pipeline, after RTP depayload
|> child(:forward_tee, Membrane.Tee.Parallel)
|> via_out(:copy1) |> get_child(:local_sink)
|> via_out(:copy2) |> get_child(:forward_output)
```

### MediaBridge Implementation
Replace TODOs in media_bridge.ex:
```elixir
defp setup_forwarding(%{a_leg: a_session, b_leg: b_session} = bridge) do
  # Bidirectional forwarding
  :ok = MediaSession.set_rtp_forward(a_session.id, %{target_session_id: b_session.id})
  :ok = MediaSession.set_rtp_forward(b_session.id, %{target_session_id: a_session.id})
  {:ok, bridge}
end
```

## Test Strategy
- Unit test: MediaSession.set_rtp_forward/2 API
- Integration test: Two media sessions, verify audio flows both directions
- SIPp test: B2BUA bridge scenario with audio verification

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] MediaSession.set_rtp_forward/2 API implemented
- [ ] Pipeline has Tee element for forwarding
- [ ] MediaBridge uses forwarding API (remove TODOs)
- [ ] Audio flows bidirectionally between bridged sessions
- [ ] Pause/resume forwarding works for hold scenarios
- [ ] B2BUA bridge tests pass with actual audio

title: [parrot_media] Pipeline terminates on file playback completion, blocking IVR/DTMF reception
description:
## Summary

When an audio file finishes playing, the media pipeline terminates completely instead of staying alive to receive incoming DTMF/audio. This breaks IVR scenarios where the caller needs to enter input after hearing a prompt.

## Root Cause

`SwitchableFileSource` emits `end_of_stream: :output` when a file completes (lines 555, 559, 589, 596). This EOS propagates through the entire pipeline chain and causes Membrane to terminate the pipeline.

**Code path:**
1. File finishes → `handle_file_complete/1` called
2. Handler returns `:noreply` (expecting DTMF)  
3. `SwitchableFileSource` emits `{[end_of_stream: :output], state}`
4. EOS cascades: timestamp_gen → encoder → chunker → tee → RTP
5. Pipeline interprets EOS as "stream complete" → terminates
6. UDP socket closes, no more RTP reception possible
7. DTMF sent by caller has no pipeline to receive it

## To Reproduce

1. Start any IVR-style handler that plays a prompt
2. Call with pjsua
3. Wait for audio to finish playing
4. Send DTMF digits
5. Observe: pipeline terminated, DTMF never received

## Expected Behavior

Pipeline should stay alive after playback to:
- Continue receiving incoming audio/DTMF
- Allow subsequent playback operations
- Only terminate on explicit stop or call hangup

## Actual Behavior

Pipeline terminates 5-10 seconds after audio ends (timestamp diff: pipeline ends at 17:27:33, DTMF sent at 17:27:51).

## Files Involved

- `apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/switchable_file_source.ex` (lines 555, 559, 589, 596)
- `apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/alaw_pipeline.ex` (lines 202-231)
- `apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/media_session.ex` (line 612-614)

## Fix Strategy

Option A: Never emit EOS from audio source except on explicit stop
- Keep `SwitchableFileSource` in "idle, waiting for commands" state
- Add `:idle` or `:wait_for_input` handler response that doesn't emit EOS

Option B: Decouple playback from pipeline lifecycle  
- Use dynamic Membrane topology to add/remove playback chains
- Main pipeline stays alive, only audio source sub-graph terminates
close_reason:
Fixed: 
1. Added notify_parent in SwitchableFileSource (commit 3669b29)
2. Added Call.Server creation in Bridge.Handler with notify_pid wiring
3. Integration test confirms IVR callbacks (handle_play_complete, handle_dtmf timeout) now fire correctly

comments.author: byoungdale
comments.text:
## Root Cause Analysis

**Problem:** IVR handler callbacks (handle_play_complete, handle_dtmf) never fire.

**Chain of events (broken):**
1. SwitchableFileSource finishes playing file
2. Calls DSL.MediaHandler.handle_play_complete() directly ✓
3. Returns {:idle, state} to stay alive for DTMF ✓
4. **MISSING:** No `notify_parent: {:file_complete, filename}` sent to AlawPipeline
5. AlawPipeline never receives the notification
6. MediaSession never gets `{:pipeline_event, :play_complete, ...}`
7. Call.Server never gets `{:media_event, ..., {:play_complete, ...}}`
8. IVRMenuHandler.handle_play_complete never called ❌

**DTMF also broken because:**
- DTMF collection requires `collect_dtmf` to be started
- `collect_dtmf` is called in `prompt()` after `handle_play_complete`
- Since `handle_play_complete` never fires, `collect_dtmf` never starts
- MediaSession ignores DTMF when not collecting (line 739-741)

**Fix required:**
In `apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/switchable_file_source.ex`, add:
```elixir
# In handle_file_complete/1, after calling handler:
{[notify_parent: {:file_complete, state.current_file}], new_state}
```

**Related bugs (duplicates):**
- parrot_platform-opx (same issue)
- parrot_platform-e8l (same issue)

comments.author: byoungdale
comments.text:
## Additional Fix Required

The `notify_parent` fix (commit 3669b29) ensures events reach MediaSession, but there's a second issue:

**Missing:** MediaSession.notify_pid is never set, so events can't reach Call.Server.

**Current flow:**
1. Bridge.Handler creates MediaSession (no notify_pid)
2. Calls handler.handle_invite() directly (synchronous)  
3. No Call.Server process exists to receive async events

**Required:**
1. Bridge.Handler must create a Call.Server for each call
2. Pass Call.Server PID as notify_pid to MediaSession

This is the second half of the fix.

title: B2BUA fork: A-leg state not updated after answer() - connect fails with :leg_not_answered
description:
## Summary
When using B2BUA forking with the DSL, the A-leg's state in the B2BUA session remains :init even after calling answer() on the call. This causes B2BUA.connect/3 to fail with :leg_not_answered.

## Reproduction Steps
1. Run: SIP_TRACE=true LOG_LEVEL=debug mix run scripts/dev/test_b2bua_fork.exs
2. Call with pjsua: pjsua --null-audio --no-tcp 'sip:fork@127.0.0.1:5080;transport=udp'
3. Observe server logs show: [error] [B2BUA-Fork] Connect failed: :leg_not_answered
4. Final leg states show a_leg: init (should be :answered)

## Root Cause
The test script creates A-leg with state: :init (line 50-52). When answer() is called on the DSL call (line 60), it sends SIP 200 OK but does not update the B2BUA's A-leg state. The Leg state machine requires: init -> trying -> answered.

## Solution Options
1. Fix test script: Create A-leg with state: :answered directly, or call B2BUA.handle_leg_event for proper state transitions
2. Production fix: Add automatic A-leg state synchronization when answer() is called in DSL context
3. Alternative: Add B2BUA.answer_a_leg/1 convenience function that handles the state transition

title: BYE request not received by server during B2BUA fork test
description:
## Summary
When pjsua sends BYE to hangup, the B2BUA fork test server does not receive the BYE request. pjsua retransmits BYE 3 times but no response is received, and the server log shows no record of receiving the BYE.

## Reproduction Steps
1. Run: SIP_TRACE=true LOG_LEVEL=debug mix run scripts/dev/test_b2bua_fork.exs
2. Call with pjsua: (sleep 5; echo h; sleep 1; echo q) | pjsua --null-audio --no-tcp 'sip:fork@127.0.0.1:5080;transport=udp'
3. Check /tmp/pjsua_fork.log - shows BYE sent at 18:24:52 with 2 retransmits
4. Check /tmp/b2bua_fork_server.log - only shows 2 SIP TRACE recv: INVITE and ACK

## Evidence
Server received only 2 messages (grep output):
- 18:24:47.243 [info] [SIP TRACE] UDP recv from 127.0.0.1:5060 (INVITE)
- 18:24:47.423 [info] [SIP TRACE] UDP recv from 127.0.0.1:5060 (ACK)

pjsua sent BYE at 18:24:52.086 (5 seconds later) with retransmissions at 18:24:52.588 and 18:24:53.591

## Possible Causes
1. Server process killed before BYE arrived (pkill timing)
2. UDP transport issue - socket not listening or blocked
3. handle_hangup callback not being invoked

## Impact
Without BYE handling, calls cannot be properly terminated by the remote party, leading to orphaned call state.

title: [CODE-REVIEW] Tests Using :sys.get_state for Internal State Inspection
description:
## Problem
10+ tests inspect internal GenServer/gen_statem state via `:sys.get_state()`.

## Locations
- `apps/parrot_transport/test/parrot_transport/connection_test.exs`: Lines 31-32, 48-49
- `apps/parrot_transport/test/parrot_transport/udp_listener_test.exs`: Line 99
- `apps/parrot_media/test/parrot_media/media_session_hold_resume_test.exs`: Lines 40-41, 73-74

## Anti-pattern
```elixir
state = :sys.get_state(pid)
assert elem(state, 0) == :connected  # Testing internal structure!
```

## Impact
- Tests brittle to refactoring
- Couples tests to internal state representation
- Breaks when gen_statem structure changes

## Fix
Add public query APIs or use behavioral assertions:
```elixir
assert Connection.connected?(pid)  # Public API
```

## Severity
P2 - Test maintainability issue
close_reason: Replaced with detailed epic parrot_platform-3op with 28 individual tasks

title: Media Forking for External Services
description:
# Media Forking for External Services

Implement fork_media/2 and stop_fork_media/2 to stream audio to external services (AI, transcription, recording).

## Business Value
- Enables AI-powered call features (transcription, sentiment, assistant)
- Supports compliance recording to external systems
- Foundation for real-time analytics and coaching

## DSL API

```elixir
# Start media fork to external service
call |> fork_media("wss://ai-service.com/stream", direction: :both)
call |> fork_media("rtp://recorder:5000", direction: :rx)

# Stop media fork
call |> stop_fork_media(fork_id)
```

## Options
- direction: :rx | :tx | :both (default: :both)
- format: :pcmu | :pcma | :opus | :raw
- label: optional identifier

## Callbacks
- handle_fork_media_connected(url, call)
- handle_fork_media_error(url, reason, call)

## Architecture

```
                    ┌─────────────────┐
                    │   Main Call     │
     A-Leg ◄───────►│   (RTP flow)    │◄───────► B-Leg
                    └────────┬────────┘
                             │ fork
                             ▼
                    ┌─────────────────┐
                    │  External Sink  │
                    │  (WebSocket/RTP)│
                    └─────────────────┘
```

## Destination Types
1. WebSocket (wss://) - For AI services like OpenAI Realtime, Google Speech
2. RTP (rtp://) - For legacy recording systems

## Dependencies
- Blocks: None (can start immediately)
- Parallel with: EPIC-DSL-002 (UPDATE), EPIC-DSL-003 (Early Media)

## Elixir Best Practices
- Pattern matching on message types, never dynamic dispatch
- Fork sinks follow Membrane Sink patterns
- Fork.Manager is GenServer under MediaSession supervision
- TDD: Write tests before implementation
- Message passing for fork control (not function calls)

```elixir
end
```

title: Create Fork.Manager GenServer
description:
Create GenServer to manage active forks in apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/fork/manager.ex

Manages fork lifecycle for a MediaSession - starting, stopping, tracking.

```elixir
defmodule ParrotMedia.Fork.Manager do
  use GenServer

  # Client API
  def start_link(opts)
  def add_fork(manager, config) :: {:ok, fork_id} | {:error, term()}
  def remove_fork(manager, fork_id) :: :ok | {:error, :not_found}
  def list_forks(manager) :: [ForkState.t()]

  # Callbacks handle:
  # - Creating appropriate sink (WebSocket or RTP based on URL)
  # - Linking to pipeline via Membrane.Pipeline.spec/2
  # - Tracking in state
  # - Event notifications to parent (Call.Server)
end
```

Acceptance:
- Start/stop forks dynamically
- Track multiple concurrent forks
- Proper cleanup on fork removal
- Event notifications to parent process
- Handle sink crashes gracefully
- Unit tests with mock pipeline

- apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/opus_pipeline.ex
- apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/pipeline/fork_tee.ex (new)

```elixir
# Pipeline flow change:
# Before: source -> decoder -> encoder -> sink
# After:  source -> decoder -> fork_tee -> encoder -> sink
#                                  │
#                                  └──> [dynamic fork sinks]

defmodule ParrotMedia.Pipeline.ForkTee do
  use Membrane.Filter

  def_output_pad :main, accepted_format: _any, flow_control: :push
  def_output_pad :fork, accepted_format: _any, flow_control: :push, availability: :on_request
  def_input_pad :input, accepted_format: _any, flow_control: :push

  # Forward to :main and all active :fork_N pads
end
```

Acceptance:
- Tee element added to both pipelines
- Dynamic fork pads can be added/removed
- No latency impact on main audio path
- Direction filtering (rx/tx/both) works
- Integration tests with existing pipeline tests
close_reason: Fork tee already implemented in alaw_pipeline.ex and opus_pipeline.ex using Membrane.Tee with dynamic fork sink support

```elixir
@doc """
```

title: Add fork callbacks to InviteHandler
Implement fork operations in ActionExecutor
description:
Add execution logic in apps/parrot/lib/parrot/call/action_executor.ex

```elixir
def execute({:fork_media, destination, direction, format, label}, state) do
  case ParrotMedia.MediaSession.fork_media(
    state.media_session_id, destination,
    direction: direction, format: format, label: label
  ) do
    {:ok, fork_id} ->
      updated_assigns = Map.put(state.assigns, {:fork, destination}, fork_id)
      {:ok, %{state | assigns: updated_assigns}}
    {:error, reason} ->
      Logger.error("Fork media failed: #{inspect(reason)}")
      {:error, reason}
  end
end

def execute({:stop_fork_media, fork_id}, state) do
  # Delegate to MediaSession
end
```

Acceptance:
- fork_media creates fork via MediaSession
- stop_fork_media removes fork
- Fork ID stored in call assigns
- Error handling follows existing patterns
- Integration tests

title: DSL SDP/Media Negotiation
description:
Enable Parrot DSL Bridge.Handler to perform proper SDP offer/answer negotiation so calls work with standard SIP clients (pjsua, linphone).

**Core Problem**: Bridge.Handler ignores SDP in INVITE body, ActionExecutor sends 200 OK with no SDP body, MediaSession is never created.

**Solution**: 
1. Bridge.Handler creates MediaSession and calls process_offer() when INVITE has SDP
2. ActionExecutor includes SDP answer in 200 OK response
3. MediaSession PID is tracked and passed through context for media operations

**Working Reference**: DTMFTestHandler shows correct pattern - create MediaSession, process_offer(), put answer in 200 OK

**Spec**: specs/008-dsl-sdp-negotiation/spec.md
**Plan**: specs/008-dsl-sdp-negotiation/plan.md
**Tasks**: specs/008-dsl-sdp-negotiation/tasks.md

title: [CODE-REVIEW] Callback Exception Masking in ExpiryManager
description:
## Problem
**Location:** `apps/parrot/lib/parrot/registration/expiry_manager.ex:254-272`

Handler callback exceptions are caught and logged instead of propagating.

## Code
```elixir
defp invoke_callback_safe(handler, aor, contact) do
  try do
    handler.handle_registration_expired(aor, contact)
  rescue
    e ->
      Logger.error("Exception in callback: ...")
      :error
  catch
    kind, reason ->
      Logger.error("Caught #{kind}: ...")
      :error
  end
end
```

## Impact
- Bugs in handler code are masked
- "Safe" wrapper defeats OTP crash-and-restart
- Harder to debug handler issues

## Fix
Let exceptions propagate or use explicit error tuples only.

## Severity
P2 - Violates OTP principles

title: Dialog State Replication Infrastructure
description:
Implement Phoenix.PubSub-based dialog state replication for cluster support (Kamailio DMQ / Phoenix.Presence style).

## Overview
Enable ParrotSip to run in a cluster where any node can handle requests for any dialog. Uses in-memory replication with async broadcast - no external dependencies.

## Architecture
```
     Node A                              Node B
┌─────────────────┐                ┌─────────────────┐
│ DialogStatem    │                │ DialogStatem    │
│ (local process) │                │ (local process) │
└────────┬────────┘                └────────┬────────┘
         │                                  │
         ▼                                  ▼
┌─────────────────┐                ┌─────────────────┐
│ DialogStore.ETS │◄── PubSub ───►│ DialogStore.ETS │
│ (full replica)  │   broadcast    │ (full replica)  │
└─────────────────┘                └─────────────────┘
```

## Key Design Decisions
1. Processes remain local (timer precision)
2. State replicated to all nodes (availability)
3. Async writes (performance)
4. Sync on dialog creation (consistency for 200 OK)
5. Behaviour pattern for pluggable backends

## Failure Handling
- Node monitor detects :nodedown
- Surviving node has full replica
- ClusterMonitor triggers takeover
- New DialogStatem spawned with recovered state

## References
- Kamailio DMQ module
- Phoenix.Presence CRDT pattern
- RFC 3261 dialog state machine
close_reason: Complete - All 13 tasks done: DialogBroadcast, ClusterMonitor, recovery capability, integration tests

title: Add phoenix_pubsub dependency
description:
Add Phoenix.PubSub to parrot_sip for inter-node communication.

## Changes

### mix.exs

title: Add integration tests for cluster failover
description:
Create integration tests that verify dialog recovery works across nodes.

## Test Scenarios

### 1. PubSub Replication Test
```elixir
test "dialog state is replicated via PubSub" do
  # Subscribe to replication topic
  Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(ParrotSip.PubSub, "dialog_replication")
  
  # Store a dialog
  dialog = build_test_dialog("replicated-1")
  DialogStore.store(dialog.id, dialog)
  
  # Should receive broadcast
  assert_receive {:dialog_stored, "replicated-1", ^dialog, _node}
end
```

### 2. Recovery Simulation Test
```elixir
test "ClusterMonitor recovers dialogs from dead node" do
  # Store a dialog owned by fake "dead" node
  dialog = build_test_dialog("orphan-1", owning_node: :fake_dead_node)
  DialogStore.store(dialog.id, dialog)
  
  # Simulate nodedown event
  send(ClusterMonitor, {:nodedown, :fake_dead_node, []})
  
  # Wait for recovery
  Process.sleep(100)
  
  # Dialog should now have a running process
  assert {:ok, pid} = DialogStatem.whereis(dialog.id)
  assert Process.alive?(pid)
  
  # Ownership should be transferred
  {:ok, updated} = DialogStore.fetch(dialog.id)
  assert updated.owning_node == node()
end
```

### 3. Multi-Node Test (requires distributed test setup)
```elixir
@tag :distributed
test "dialog survives node failure" do
  # This test requires LocalCluster or similar
  # Start two nodes
  [node1, node2] = LocalCluster.start_nodes("parrot", 2)
  
  # Create dialog on node1
  dialog_id = :rpc.call(node1, TestHelper, :create_dialog, [])
  
  # Verify node2 has replica
  assert {:ok, _} = :rpc.call(node2, DialogStore, :fetch, [dialog_id])
  
  # Kill node1
  LocalCluster.stop_nodes([node1])
  
  # Wait for recovery
  Process.sleep(500)
  
  # Dialog should be recovered on node2
  assert {:ok, pid} = :rpc.call(node2, DialogStatem, :whereis, [dialog_id])
  assert :rpc.call(node2, Process, :alive?, [pid])
end
```

title: Implement DialogStore.ETS (local-only)
description:
Create the ETS-based DialogStore implementation for single-node deployments.

## Purpose
Simple, fast implementation for:
- Development and testing
- Single-node production deployments
- Base layer for Replicated implementation

## Implementation

```elixir
defmodule ParrotSip.DialogStore.ETS do
  @behaviour ParrotSip.DialogStore
  
  use GenServer
  
  @table __MODULE__
  
  def start_link(opts) do
    GenServer.start_link(__MODULE__, opts, name: __MODULE__)
  end
  
  def init(_opts) do
    table = :ets.new(@table, [:named_table, :public, :set, read_concurrency: true])
    {:ok, %{table: table}}
  end
  
  # Sync store
  @impl true
  def store(dialog_id, state) do
    record = {dialog_id, state, node(), DateTime.utc_now()}
    :ets.insert(@table, record)
    :ok
  end
  
  # Async store (same as sync for ETS, but matches behaviour)
  @impl true
  def store_async(dialog_id, state) do
    store(dialog_id, state)
  end
  
  @impl true
  def fetch(dialog_id) do
    case :ets.lookup(@table, dialog_id) do
      [{^dialog_id, state, _node, _time}] -> {:ok, state}
      [] -> {:error, :not_found}
    end
  end
  
  @impl true
  def delete(dialog_id) do
    :ets.delete(@table, dialog_id)
    :ok
  end
  
  @impl true
  def list_by_node(target_node) do
    :ets.select(@table, [{{:_, :\, target_node, :_}, [], [:\]}])
  end
  
  @impl true
  def list_all do
    :ets.select(@table, [{{:_, :\, :_, :_}, [], [:\]}])
  end
  
  @impl true
  def transfer_ownership(dialog_id, new_node) do
    case fetch(dialog_id) do
      {:ok, state} ->
        updated = %{state | owning_node: new_node, updated_at: DateTime.utc_now()}
        store(dialog_id, updated)
      error -> error
    end
  end
end
```

- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/dialog_store/ets.ex

## Tests to add
- Store and fetch dialog
- Delete dialog
- List by node
- Transfer ownership
- Concurrent access

title: Implement DialogStore.Replicated (PubSub broadcast)
description:
Create the PubSub-based DialogStore implementation for multi-node clusters.

## Purpose
Enable dialog state replication across cluster nodes using Phoenix.PubSub broadcast (similar to Kamailio DMQ).

## Architecture
```
Local writes ──► ETS (local) ──► PubSub broadcast ──► Other nodes update their ETS
```

## Implementation

```elixir
defmodule ParrotSip.DialogStore.Replicated do
  @behaviour ParrotSip.DialogStore
  
  use GenServer
  
  @table :parrot_dialog_store
  @pubsub ParrotSip.PubSub
  @topic "dialog_replication"
  
  def start_link(opts) do
    GenServer.start_link(__MODULE__, opts, name: __MODULE__)
  end
  
  def init(_opts) do
    # Create ETS table for local replica
    table = :ets.new(@table, [:named_table, :public, :set, read_concurrency: true])
    
    # Subscribe to replication topic
    Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(@pubsub, @topic)
    
    {:ok, %{table: table}}
  end
  
  # Sync store - write locally then broadcast
  @impl true
  def store(dialog_id, state) do
    record = {dialog_id, state, node(), DateTime.utc_now()}
    :ets.insert(@table, record)
    
    # Broadcast to other nodes (async)
    broadcast({:dialog_stored, dialog_id, state, node()})
    :ok
  end
  
  # Async store via GenServer cast
  @impl true  
  def store_async(dialog_id, state) do
    GenServer.cast(__MODULE__, {:store_async, dialog_id, state})
    :ok
  end
  
  def handle_cast({:store_async, dialog_id, state}, state_data) do
    store(dialog_id, state)
    {:noreply, state_data}
  end
  
  @impl true
  def fetch(dialog_id) do
    case :ets.lookup(@table, dialog_id) do
      [{^dialog_id, state, _node, _time}] -> {:ok, state}
      [] -> {:error, :not_found}
    end
  end
  
  @impl true
  def delete(dialog_id) do
    :ets.delete(@table, dialog_id)
    broadcast({:dialog_deleted, dialog_id, node()})
    :ok
  end
  
  @impl true
  def list_by_node(target_node) do
    :ets.select(@table, [{{:_, :\, target_node, :_}, [], [:\]}])
  end
  
  @impl true
  def list_all do
    :ets.select(@table, [{{:_, :\, :_, :_}, [], [:\]}])
  end
  
  @impl true
  def transfer_ownership(dialog_id, new_node) do
    case fetch(dialog_id) do
      {:ok, state} ->
        updated = %{state | owning_node: new_node, updated_at: DateTime.utc_now()}
        store(dialog_id, updated)
      error -> error
    end
  end
  
  # Handle incoming replication messages from other nodes
  def handle_info({:dialog_stored, dialog_id, state, from_node}, state_data) do
    # Don't process our own broadcasts
    if from_node \!= node() do
      record = {dialog_id, state, from_node, DateTime.utc_now()}
      :ets.insert(@table, record)
    end
    {:noreply, state_data}
  end
  
  def handle_info({:dialog_deleted, dialog_id, from_node}, state_data) do
    if from_node \!= node() do
      :ets.delete(@table, dialog_id)
    end
    {:noreply, state_data}
  end
  
  defp broadcast(message) do
    Phoenix.PubSub.broadcast(@pubsub, @topic, message)
  end
end
```

- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/dialog_store/replicated.ex

- Local store and fetch
- Broadcast received by other subscribers
- Ignore self-broadcasts
- Delete propagation

title: Implement ClusterMonitor for node failure detection
description:
Create ClusterMonitor GenServer that detects node failures and triggers dialog recovery.

## Purpose
Detect when cluster nodes fail and trigger takeover of orphaned dialogs.

## Implementation

```elixir
defmodule ParrotSip.ClusterMonitor do
  @moduledoc """
  Monitors cluster nodes and triggers dialog recovery on node failure.
  
  When a node goes down:
  1. Query DialogStore for dialogs owned by dead node
  2. For each orphaned dialog, spawn a recovered DialogStatem
  3. Update ownership in DialogStore
  """
  
  use GenServer
  require Logger
  
  def start_link(opts) do
    GenServer.start_link(__MODULE__, opts, name: __MODULE__)
  end
  
  def init(_opts) do
    # Monitor all nodes in the cluster
    :net_kernel.monitor_nodes(true, [:nodedown_reason])
    
    # Get current cluster state
    nodes = Node.list()
    Logger.info("ClusterMonitor started, connected to \#{length(nodes)} nodes")
    
    {:ok, %{nodes: nodes}}
  end
  
  def handle_info({:nodeup, node, _info}, state) do
    Logger.info("ClusterMonitor: Node joined: \#{node}")
    
    # Could trigger state sync here if needed
    
    {:noreply, %{state | nodes: [node | state.nodes]}}
  end
  
  def handle_info({:nodedown, dead_node, info}, state) do
    Logger.warning("ClusterMonitor: Node down: \#{dead_node}, reason: \#{inspect(info)}")
    
    # Recover dialogs owned by dead node
    recover_dialogs_from_node(dead_node)
    
    {:noreply, %{state | nodes: List.delete(state.nodes, dead_node)}}
  end
  
  defp recover_dialogs_from_node(dead_node) do
    orphaned_dialogs = ParrotSip.DialogStore.list_by_node(dead_node)
    
    Logger.info("ClusterMonitor: Recovering \#{length(orphaned_dialogs)} dialogs from \#{dead_node}")
    
    for dialog_state <- orphaned_dialogs do
      case ParrotSip.DialogStatem.start_recovered(dialog_state) do
        {:ok, pid} ->
          Logger.info("ClusterMonitor: Recovered dialog \#{dialog_state.id} as \#{inspect(pid)}")
          ParrotSip.DialogStore.transfer_ownership(dialog_state.id, node())
          
        {:error, reason} ->
          Logger.error("ClusterMonitor: Failed to recover dialog \#{dialog_state.id}: \#{inspect(reason)}")
      end
    end
  end
end
```

## Files to create
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cluster_monitor.ex

## Files to modify
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/application.ex (add to supervision tree)

## Tests
- Test nodedown handling triggers recovery
- Test recovered dialogs are functional
- Test ownership transfer

## Notes
- Only one node should recover each dialog (use distributed lock or leader election for large clusters)
- For simple 2-3 node clusters, race conditions are acceptable (duplicate recovery is idempotent)

title: Add dialog recovery capability to DialogStatem
description:
Add the ability to start a DialogStatem from recovered state.

## Purpose
When a node fails, ClusterMonitor needs to spawn DialogStatem processes from stored state.

## Changes to DialogStatem

### Add start_recovered/1 function
```elixir
@doc """
Start a DialogStatem from recovered state (used during node failure recovery).
"""
@spec start_recovered(map()) :: GenStatem.on_start()
def start_recovered(stored_state) do
  ParrotSip.Dialog.Supervisor.start_child({:recover, stored_state})
end
```

### Add recovery init clause
```elixir
def init({:recover, stored_state}) do
  # Deserialize stored state back to Dialog struct
  dialog = deserialize_dialog(stored_state)
  
  # Re-register in local registry
  case Registry.register(ParrotSip.Registry, dialog.id, self()) do
    {:ok, _} -> :ok
    {:error, {:already_registered, _}} ->
      # Another process already recovered this dialog
      Logger.warning("Dialog \#{dialog.id} already recovered by another process")
      {:stop, :already_recovered}
  end
  
  # Rebuild any necessary timers based on state
  timers = rebuild_timers(dialog)
  
  Logger.info("[DialogStatem] Recovered dialog \#{dialog.id} in state \#{dialog.state}")
  
  # Notify owner if configured
  notify_owner_of_recovery(dialog)
  
  {:ok, dialog.state, %{
    dialog: dialog,
    timers: timers,
    recovered: true,
    recovered_at: DateTime.utc_now()
  }}
end

defp deserialize_dialog(stored_state) do
  %ParrotSip.Dialog{
    id: stored_state.id,
    state: stored_state.state,
    call_id: stored_state.call_id,
    local_tag: stored_state.local_tag,
    remote_tag: stored_state.remote_tag,
    local_uri: stored_state.local_uri,
    remote_uri: stored_state.remote_uri,
    remote_target: stored_state.remote_target,
    local_seq: stored_state.local_seq,
    remote_seq: stored_state.remote_seq,
    route_set: stored_state.route_set
  }
end

defp rebuild_timers(%{state: :confirmed} = dialog) do
  # In confirmed state, we might need session timers
  # RFC 4028 Session-Expires handling
  []
end

defp rebuild_timers(_dialog) do
  []
end

defp notify_owner_of_recovery(dialog) do
  # If there's an owner process registered, notify it
  case Registry.lookup(ParrotSip.Registry, {:dialog_owner, dialog.id}) do
    [{owner_pid, _}] ->
      send(owner_pid, {:dialog_recovered, dialog.id, self()})
    [] ->
      :ok
  end
end
```

- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/dialog_statem.ex

## Tests
- Test start_recovered/1 creates functional dialog
- Test registry registration on recovery
- Test duplicate recovery is handled gracefully
- Test timers are rebuilt correctly

title: Integrate DialogStore persistence into DialogStatem
description:
Modify DialogStatem to persist state changes to DialogStore.

## Purpose
Ensure dialog state is replicated to other nodes by writing to DialogStore on state changes.

## Changes to DialogStatem

### Persist on dialog creation (init)
```elixir
def init({:uas, request, response}) do
  {:ok, dialog} = Dialog.uas_create(request, response)
  
  # SYNC persist - must complete before we continue
  # This ensures state is replicated before 200 OK is sent
  :ok = DialogStore.store(dialog.id, serialize_dialog(dialog))
  
  # Register in local registry
  Registry.register(ParrotSip.Registry, dialog.id, self())
  
  {:ok, :confirmed, %{dialog: dialog}}
end
```

### Persist on state transitions (async)
```elixir
defp persist_state_change(dialog) do
  # ASYNC persist - don't block the state machine
  DialogStore.store_async(dialog.id, serialize_dialog(dialog))
end

# Example: handling in-dialog request
def confirmed(:cast, {:uas_request, msg}, data) do
  {:ok, updated_dialog} = Dialog.uas_process(msg, data.dialog)
  
  # Async persist
  persist_state_change(updated_dialog)
  
  {:keep_state, %{data | dialog: updated_dialog}}
end
```

### Delete on termination
```elixir
def terminate(_reason, _state, data) do
  DialogStore.delete(data.dialog.id)
  :ok
end
```

### Serialization helper
```elixir
defp serialize_dialog(%Dialog{} = dialog) do
  %{
    id: dialog.id,
    state: dialog.state,
    call_id: dialog.call_id,
    local_tag: dialog.local_tag,
    remote_tag: dialog.remote_tag,
    local_uri: dialog.local_uri,
    remote_uri: dialog.remote_uri,
    remote_target: dialog.remote_target,
    local_seq: dialog.local_seq,
    remote_seq: dialog.remote_seq,
    route_set: dialog.route_set,
    owning_node: node(),
    created_at: dialog.created_at || DateTime.utc_now(),
    updated_at: DateTime.utc_now()
  }
end
```

## Files to modify
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/dialog_statem.ex

## Key Points
1. Dialog creation: SYNC write (must be stored before proceeding)
2. State updates: ASYNC write (don't block state machine)
3. Termination: Delete from store

## Tests
- Test dialog creation persists to store
- Test state changes are persisted
- Test termination deletes from store
- Test async writes don't block state machine

description:
Create comprehensive unit tests for DialogStore behaviour and implementations.

## Test Files to Create

### test/parrot_sip/dialog_store_test.exs
Test the behaviour contract with both implementations.

```elixir
defmodule ParrotSip.DialogStoreTest do
  use ExUnit.Case, async: false
  
  # Test both implementations
  for impl <- [ParrotSip.DialogStore.ETS, ParrotSip.DialogStore.Replicated] do
    @impl impl
    
    describe "\#{@impl}" do
      setup do
        # Start the implementation
        start_supervised!(@impl)
        :ok
      end
      
      test "store and fetch dialog" do
        dialog = build_test_dialog("dialog-1")
        
        assert :ok = @impl.store(dialog.id, dialog)
        assert {:ok, ^dialog} = @impl.fetch(dialog.id)
      end
      
      test "fetch non-existent dialog returns error" do
        assert {:error, :not_found} = @impl.fetch("non-existent")
      end
      
      test "delete dialog" do
        dialog = build_test_dialog("dialog-2")
        :ok = @impl.store(dialog.id, dialog)
        
        assert :ok = @impl.delete(dialog.id)
        assert {:error, :not_found} = @impl.fetch(dialog.id)
      end
      
      test "list_by_node returns dialogs for specific node" do
        dialog1 = build_test_dialog("d1", owning_node: :node_a)
        dialog2 = build_test_dialog("d2", owning_node: :node_b)
        dialog3 = build_test_dialog("d3", owning_node: :node_a)
        
        :ok = @impl.store(dialog1.id, dialog1)
        :ok = @impl.store(dialog2.id, dialog2)
        :ok = @impl.store(dialog3.id, dialog3)
        
        node_a_dialogs = @impl.list_by_node(:node_a)
        assert length(node_a_dialogs) == 2
      end
      
      test "transfer_ownership updates owning_node" do
        dialog = build_test_dialog("d4", owning_node: :node_a)
        :ok = @impl.store(dialog.id, dialog)
        
        :ok = @impl.transfer_ownership(dialog.id, :node_b)
        
        {:ok, updated} = @impl.fetch(dialog.id)
        assert updated.owning_node == :node_b
      end
      
      test "store_async completes without blocking" do
        dialog = build_test_dialog("d5")
        
        # Should return immediately
        assert :ok = @impl.store_async(dialog.id, dialog)
        
        # Give async operation time to complete
        Process.sleep(10)
        
        assert {:ok, _} = @impl.fetch(dialog.id)
      end
    end
  end
  
  defp build_test_dialog(id, opts \\ []) do
    %{
      id: id,
      state: :confirmed,
      call_id: "call-\#{id}",
      local_tag: "local-tag",
      remote_tag: "remote-tag",
      local_uri: "sip:local@example.com",
      remote_uri: "sip:remote@example.com",
      remote_target: "sip:remote@192.168.1.1",
      local_seq: 1,
      remote_seq: 1,
      route_set: [],
      owning_node: Keyword.get(opts, :owning_node, node()),
      created_at: DateTime.utc_now(),
      updated_at: DateTime.utc_now()
    }
  end
end
```

title: CDR System - Call Detail Records for Parrot Platform
description:
Implement Call Detail Record (CDR) system that automatically generates CDR structs for every INVITE dialog, delivering them to user-implemented handlers via a behaviour pattern.

## Summary
- Hooks into DialogStatem termination to capture all calls
- Provides CDR struct, generator, handler behaviour, dispatcher, and serializers
- Library pattern: no built-in storage, handlers own persistence
- Fire-and-forget handler delivery

## User Stories
1. US1 (P1): Automatic CDR Generation - CDRs generated for every call
2. US2 (P2): CDR Retrieval and Query - Query filter helpers
3. US3 (P3): CDR Export - JSON/CSV serialization
4. US4 (P4): Custom CDR Handler - Handler behaviour and examples

## Key Files
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr.ex
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr/generator.ex
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr/handler.ex
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr/dispatcher.ex
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr/serializer.ex
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/dialog_statem.ex (modified)

## Design Docs
- specs/006-cdr-system/spec.md
- specs/006-cdr-system/plan.md
- specs/006-cdr-system/data-model.md
- specs/006-cdr-system/research.md

title: Implement CDR.TerminationCause struct
description:
Implement CDR.TerminationCause struct (tests must pass).

## File Path
apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr/termination_cause.ex

## Implementation
```elixir
defmodule ParrotSip.CDR.TerminationCause do
  @moduledoc """
  Describes the termination cause of a call.
  """

  @type party :: :caller | :callee | :system
  @type method :: :bye | :cancel | :error | nil

  @type t :: %__MODULE__{
          party: party(),
          sip_code: non_neg_integer(),
          reason: String.t(),
          method: method()
        }

  defstruct [:party, :sip_code, :reason, :method]
end
```

## Verification

- mix compile --warnings-as-errors succeeds

description:

## File Path

## Test Cases (from research.md mapping table)
- 200-299 after answer → :answered
- 486 → :busy
- 480 → :no_answer
- 408 → :timeout
- 487 → :cancelled
- 603 → :declined
- 404, 604 → :not_found
- 403, 401, 407 → :forbidden
- 500-599 → :server_error
- Other 4xx → :failed
- 3xx → :redirected
- No response → :abandoned

## Function to Test
from_sip_code(code, dialog_state) → disposition atom

title: Implement disposition mapping module
description:
Implement disposition mapping module (tests must pass).

## File Path
apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr/disposition.ex

## Implementation
Pattern matching on SIP response codes to return disposition atoms.

```elixir
defmodule ParrotSip.CDR.Disposition do
  @moduledoc """
  Maps SIP response codes to CDR disposition atoms.
  """

  @spec from_sip_code(non_neg_integer(), :early | :confirmed) :: atom()
  def from_sip_code(code, dialog_state)
  
  # 2xx with confirmed dialog = answered
  def from_sip_code(code, :confirmed) when code >= 200 and code < 300, do: :answered
  
  # Specific codes
  def from_sip_code(486, _), do: :busy
  def from_sip_code(480, _), do: :no_answer
  # ... etc
end
```

## Verification
- mix test test/parrot_sip/cdr/disposition_test.exs passes

title: Phase 3: US1 - Automatic CDR Generation (P1)
description:
CORE FUNCTIONALITY: CDRs automatically generated for every call upon termination.

## User Story
As a platform operator, I want call detail records to be automatically generated for every call so that I have complete records for billing, compliance, and analytics.

## Independent Test
Complete a call via SIPp, verify CDR generated with all mandatory fields.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. CDR created for completed inbound/outbound calls
2. CDR created for failed/rejected calls  
3. CDR reflects actual talk time and termination cause
4. CDR generated for every INVITE (including immediate cancels)

## Key Implementation
- CDR.Generator: Generate CDR from Dialog + timing data
- CDR.Handler: Behaviour for custom handlers
- CDR.Dispatcher: Fire-and-forget Task dispatch to handlers
- DialogStatem: Add timing fields, trigger CDR on termination

## Files Created/Modified
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr/generator.ex (new)
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr/handler.ex (new)
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr/dispatcher.ex (new)
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/dialog_statem.ex (modified)

## Performance Requirements
- SC-006: CDR generation < 10ms latency
- SC-005: Handler delivery < 100ms

title: [US1] Implement Generator role-based field mapping
description:
Implement role-based field mapping in Generator.

## File Path
apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr/generator.ex

## Mapping Logic
UAC (caller made the call):
- caller_uri = dialog.local_uri
- caller_tag = dialog.local_tag
- callee_uri = dialog.remote_uri
- callee_tag = dialog.remote_tag
- direction = :outbound

UAS (callee received the call):
- caller_uri = dialog.remote_uri
- caller_tag = dialog.remote_tag
- callee_uri = dialog.local_uri
- callee_tag = dialog.local_tag
- direction = :inbound

## Implementation
Private functions to extract fields based on role:
```elixir
defp extract_parties(dialog, :uac), do: {...}
defp extract_parties(dialog, :uas), do: {...}
```

## Context
See data-model.md Field Mapping from Dialog section.

title: [US1] Implement Generator timing calculations
description:
Implement timing calculations in Generator.

## File Path
apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr/generator.ex

## Calculations
```elixir
ended_at = DateTime.utc_now()

# Ring duration
ring_duration_ms = 
  if answered_at do
    DateTime.diff(answered_at, invite_received_at, :millisecond)
  else
    DateTime.diff(ended_at, invite_received_at, :millisecond)
  end

# Talk duration  
talk_duration_ms =
  if answered_at do
    DateTime.diff(ended_at, answered_at, :millisecond)
  else
    0
  end
```

## Edge Cases
- Call never answered: ring_duration = total time, talk_duration = 0
- Immediate cancel: ring_duration very small, talk_duration = 0

## Context
See research.md Section 5 for timing strategy.

title: [US1] Implement CDR.Handler behaviour
[US1] Implement CDR.Dispatcher with fire-and-forget Task dispatch
description:
Implement CDR.Dispatcher module.

## File Path
apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr/dispatcher.ex

## Implementation
```elixir
defmodule ParrotSip.CDR.Dispatcher do
  @moduledoc """
  Dispatches CDRs to registered handlers.
  """
  
  require Logger

  @spec dispatch(ParrotSip.CDR.t(), [{module(), term()}]) :: :ok
  def dispatch(cdr, handlers) do
    for {module, state} <- handlers do
      Task.start(fn ->
        try do
          module.handle_cdr(cdr, state)
        rescue
          e ->
            Logger.error("CDR handler #\{inspect(module)} failed: #\{Exception.message(e)}")
        end
      end)
    end
    :ok
  end
end
```

## Key Points
- Task.start/1 for fire-and-forget
- try/rescue around handler call
- Log errors but don't propagate
- Returns :ok immediately

## Context
See research.md Section 8 for error handling.

title: [US1] Implement handler registration functions

## Functions to Implement
```elixir
# Register a handler
@spec register_handler(module(), term()) :: :ok | {:error, term()}
def register_handler(handler_module, args \\ %{})

# Unregister a handler  
@spec unregister_handler(module()) :: :ok
def unregister_handler(handler_module)

# List registered handlers
@spec list_handlers() :: [{module(), term()}]
def list_handlers()
```

## Storage Options
1. Application environment (simplest)
2. ETS table (better for concurrent access)
3. Agent/GenServer (full lifecycle management)

Recommend ETS table for simplicity and performance.

## Usage
```elixir
ParrotSip.CDR.register_handler(MyCDRHandler, repo: MyApp.Repo)
```

description:

## User Story

## Independent Test

## Note

title: [US2] Write tests for CDR.build_query_filter/1
description:
Write tests for CDR.build_query_filter/1 helper.

## File Path
apps/parrot_sip/test/parrot_sip/cdr_test.exs (add to existing file)

## Test Cases
1. Empty opts returns empty map
2. start_date option produces {:>=, datetime} filter
3. end_date option produces {:<, datetime} filter
4. caller option produces string filter
5. callee option produces string filter
6. disposition single atom produces {:==, atom} filter
7. disposition list produces {:in, [atoms]} filter
8. call_id produces exact match filter
9. correlation_id produces exact match filter
10. Multiple options combined correctly

## Example Expected Outputs
```elixir
build_query_filter(start_date: ~U[2026-01-10 00:00:00Z])
# => %{invite_received_at: {:>=, ~U[2026-01-10 00:00:00Z]}}

build_query_filter(disposition: [:answered, :busy])
# => %{disposition: {:in, [:answered, :busy]}}
```

title: [US2] Implement CDR.build_query_filter/1 helper
description:
Implement CDR.build_query_filter/1 for handler query support.

## File Path
apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/cdr.ex

## Function
```elixir
@spec build_query_filter(keyword()) :: map()
def build_query_filter(opts) when is_list(opts) do
  opts
  |> Enum.reduce(%{}, fn
    {:start_date, dt}, acc -> Map.put(acc, :invite_received_at, {:>=, dt})
    {:end_date, dt}, acc -> Map.put(acc, :invite_received_at, {:<, dt})
    {:caller, uri}, acc -> Map.put(acc, :caller_uri, uri)
    {:callee, uri}, acc -> Map.put(acc, :callee_uri, uri)
    {:disposition, d}, acc when is_atom(d) -> Map.put(acc, :disposition, {:==, d})
    {:disposition, ds}, acc when is_list(ds) -> Map.put(acc, :disposition, {:in, ds})
    {:call_id, id}, acc -> Map.put(acc, :call_id, id)
    {:correlation_id, id}, acc -> Map.put(acc, :correlation_id, id)
    _, acc -> acc
  end)
end
```

## Note
This is a helper for handlers to build queries. The actual query
execution is the handler's responsibility.

title: T04: Implement fetch_mos_summary in DialogStatem.terminate
description:
Implement MOS data fetching in DialogStatem terminate callback for CDR generation.

**File:** apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/dialog_statem.ex

**Implementation:**
Add these private functions:
```elixir
defp fetch_mos_summary(%{mos_fetcher: nil}), do: nil
defp fetch_mos_summary(%{media_session_id: nil}), do: nil
defp fetch_mos_summary(%{mos_fetcher: fetcher, media_session_id: session_id}) do
  try do
    fetcher.(session_id)
  rescue
    _ -> nil  # Don't let MOS failures break CDR generation
  end
end

defp build_media_info(data, mos_summary) do
  %MediaInfo{
    codec: data.negotiated_codec,
    codec_payload_type: data.payload_type,
    mos_summary: mos_summary,
    packets_sent: get_in(mos_summary, [:total_packets]),
    packets_received: calculate_received(mos_summary),
    jitter_ms: extract_avg_jitter(mos_summary)
  }
end
```

**Error Handling:**
- try/rescue ensures MOS failures never prevent CDR generation
- CDR with mos_summary: nil is always better than no CDR

title: T06: Inject mos_fetcher in Bridge.Handler
description:
Wire up the MOS fetcher callback in the Parrot DSL Bridge.Handler when creating dialogs.

**File:** apps/parrot/lib/parrot/bridge/handler.ex

**Implementation:**
```elixir
def handle_request(%Message{method: "INVITE"} = request, state) do
  # ... existing dialog creation ...
  
  dialog_opts = [
    # ... existing opts ...
    mos_fetcher: &mos_fetcher/1  # Inject the callback
  ]
  
  DialogStatem.start_link(dialog_opts)
end

# Wrapper that converts CallSummary struct to plain map
defp mos_fetcher(session_id) do
  case ParrotMedia.MOS.call_summary(session_id) do
    {:ok, %ParrotMedia.MOS.CallSummary{} = summary} ->
      ParrotMedia.MOS.CallSummary.to_map(summary)
    {:error, _} ->
      nil
  end
end
```

**Key Points:**
- Bridge.Handler is in parrot app which has access to both parrot_sip and parrot_media
- Convert CallSummary struct to plain map at the boundary
- Return nil on any error

title: T07: Write integration tests for MOS-CDR
description:
Write comprehensive tests for the MOS-CDR integration.

**Test Files:**
- apps/parrot_sip/test/parrot_sip/cdr/media_info_test.exs
- apps/parrot_sip/test/parrot_sip/dialog_statem_cdr_test.exs (update existing)

**Unit Tests (MediaInfo):**
- mos_summary accepts full MOS summary map
- mos_summary handles nil gracefully
- Serializer produces correct JSON structure with mos_summary
- Serializer produces correct CSV columns

**Integration Tests (DialogStatem CDR):**
```elixir
test "CDR includes MOS summary when fetcher provided" do
  mock_summary = %{avg_mos: 4.2, min_mos: 3.8, max_mos: 4.4, ...}
  mos_fetcher = fn _session_id -> mock_summary end
  
  {:ok, dialog} = DialogStatem.start_link(mos_fetcher: mos_fetcher, ...)
  # ... trigger call flow and termination ...
  
  assert_receive {:cdr_generated, %CDR{} = cdr}
  assert cdr.media_info.mos_summary.avg_mos == 4.2
end

test "CDR has nil mos_summary when fetcher returns nil"
test "CDR generated even if mos_fetcher crashes"
```

**Key Principle:** Inject mock functions, don't mock parrot_media directly.

title: Implement ACK SDP Answer Bodies
description:
## Problem
When 2xx response contains SDP offer, ACK MUST carry SDP answer in body. Currently ACK is sent with empty body.

## Root Cause
transaction/client.ex:120-123 has TODO comment acknowledging this gap. ACK is auto-generated without checking if 2xx has SDP offer.

## Files to Modify
- `/apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/transaction/client.ex`
  - Lines 114-153: Auto-ACK generation for 2xx
  - Add SDP offer detection and answer generation

- `/apps/parrot_media/lib/parrot_media/sdp.ex`
  - Reuse existing process_offer_and_answer/2 function

## Implementation (Elixir Best Practices)
1. Detect SDP offer in 2xx response:
```elixir
defp has_sdp_offer?(response) do
  response.content_type == "application/sdp" and 
  response.body != nil and 
  response.body != ""
end
```

2. Generate SDP answer using ParrotMedia.Sdp:
```elixir
defp generate_ack_with_sdp(response, transaction, supported_codecs) do
  case ParrotMedia.Sdp.process_offer_and_answer(response.body, 
         supported_codecs: supported_codecs) do
    {:ok, sdp_answer, _remote_info} ->
      ack = build_ack(response, transaction)
      Message.set_body(ack, sdp_answer, "application/sdp")
    {:error, reason} ->
      Logger.warning("Failed to generate SDP answer: #{inspect(reason)}")
      build_ack(response, transaction)  # Send ACK without body
  end
end
```

3. Pass codec preferences through transaction or dialog context

## Test Strategy
- SIPp scenario: INVITE with offer → 200 OK with offer → verify ACK has answer
- Test re-INVITE without SDP → 200 with offer → ACK with answer
- Verify Content-Type and Content-Length headers set correctly

## RFC Reference
RFC 3261 Section 13.2.2.4: "If the 2xx contains an offer (based on the rules above), the ACK MUST carry an answer in its body."

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Detect SDP offer in 2xx response
- [ ] Generate SDP answer using ParrotMedia.Sdp
- [ ] Set ACK body with SDP answer
- [ ] Set Content-Type: application/sdp header
- [ ] Remove TODO comment after implementation
- [ ] SIPp re-INVITE scenarios pass

title: [test_answer_play] Membrane UnknownChildError - audio_source child doesn't exist
description:
## Description
When running test_answer_play, the AlawPipeline crashes trying to notify a child named :audio_source that doesn't exist.

## Error
```
[error] <0.317.0>/ Child of name :audio_source doesn't exist. Available children are [:rtp, :udp_endpoint].
```

## Stack Trace
- Membrane.Core.Parent.ChildrenModel.get_child_data!/2
- Membrane.Core.Parent.ChildLifeController.handle_notify_child/2
- Membrane.Core.Pipeline.ActionHandler.handle_action/4

## Root Cause Analysis
The pipeline is trying to notify :audio_source after a play action, but the AlawPipeline doesn't have this child configured. The SwitchableFileSource component may not be properly added to the pipeline.

## To Reproduce
```bash
mix run scripts/dev/test_answer_play.exs &
(sleep 4; echo h; sleep 1; echo q) | pjsua --null-audio --no-tcp --local-port=5100 'sip:test@127.0.0.1:5080'
```

title: [check_test.sh] Integer expression error on line 183
description:
The check_test.sh script fails with 'integer expression expected' error on line 183.

**Root Cause:** The grep command outputs multiple lines (0 on separate lines), causing the integer comparison to fail.

**Fix:** Change line 90 to use head -1:
```bash
RESPONSE_5XX=$(grep -cE 'SIP/2\.0 5[0-9]{2}' "$LOG_FILE" 2>/dev/null | head -1 || echo "0")
```

**File:** scripts/dev/check_test.sh:183

title: Implement Dialog Replication and BYE on Hangup
description:
Enable proper call termination by implementing dialog state replication (Phoenix.PubSub / DMQ style) and making hangup() send BYE requests.

## Architecture Decision
- Dialogs: ParrotSip manages with in-memory replication via Phoenix.PubSub
- Registrations: User application manages via RegistrationHandler callbacks (already implemented)

## Key Changes
1. Transaction.Server.response/2 returns {:ok, final_response} so caller knows the To tag
2. Add Phoenix.PubSub-based dialog replication for cluster support
3. Bridge.ActionExecutor captures dialog_id after answer
4. execute_hangup looks up dialog and sends BYE via Transaction.Client

## References
- Similar to Kamailio DMQ dialog replication
- Phoenix.Presence pattern for CRDT-style replication

title: Modify Transaction.Server.response/2 to return final response
description:
Change the API of Transaction.Server.response/2 to return {:ok, final_response} instead of :ok.

## Why
When sending a 2xx response to INVITE, the transaction layer adds a To tag (line 200 in transaction/server.ex). The caller needs to know this tag to compute the dialog_id.

## Current API
```elixir
:ok = Transaction.Server.response(response, uas)
```

## New API
```elixir
{:ok, final_response} = Transaction.Server.response(response, uas)
# final_response.to.parameters["tag"] contains the generated tag
```

## Files to modify
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/transaction/server.ex - Change response/2 return value
- apps/parrot_sip/lib/parrot_sip/transaction_statem.ex - Propagate return value
- Update all callers in parrot_sip that call Transaction.Server.response/2

## Implementation notes
- The tag is already generated in make_reply/3 (lines 190-208)
- Need to return the modified response after tag is added
- This is a breaking API change but the right design

title: Add Phoenix.PubSub dependency and dialog replication infrastructure
description:
Add Phoenix.PubSub-based dialog state replication for cluster support.

## Architecture (Kamailio DMQ / Phoenix.Presence style)
- Each node maintains local dialog state in ETS
- State changes broadcast to all nodes via PubSub
- On node failure, surviving nodes have full replica to take over

## Components to add

### 1. Add phoenix_pubsub dependency
```elixir
# in apps/parrot_sip/mix.exs
{:phoenix_pubsub, "~> 2.1"}
```

### 2. DialogStore behaviour
```elixir
defmodule ParrotSip.DialogStore do
  @callback store(dialog_id, dialog_state) :: :ok
  @callback fetch(dialog_id) :: {:ok, dialog_state} | {:error, :not_found}
  @callback delete(dialog_id) :: :ok
  @callback list_by_node(node) :: [dialog_state]
end
```

### 3. Replicated implementation (default)
